text: based on the book: “The paranormal, it turns out, is as much about meaning as matter. And we—not as surface egos, but as some still mysterious force of consciousness—are its final authors. If the paranormal, though, is as much about meaning as matter, as much about the subject as the object, then science can never truly grasp it, for science must turn everything into an object and cannot treat questions of meaning. We thus need a new way of knowing, a way that can embrace both the sciences and a new art of reading ourselves writing ourselves.” Whether or not they are provable in a laboratory setting, anomalous experiences remain a part of life for a surprising number of people. Gallup polls show that 17 percent of the population in the United States claim to have had a UFO experience. The Baylor Religion Survey, as detailed in Paranormal America , by Christopher Bader, F. Carson Mencken, and Joseph O. Baker shows that in the United States, 45 percent of women and 32 percent of men believe in the existence of ghosts, and 31 percent of women and 28 percent of men believe in telekinesis. If you take into account the various categories of paranormal possibilities, close to half of the population believes in, or claims to have experienced, something out of the ordinary in their lives. These statistics show that the paranormal is a significant motivating factor in how our culture develops and understands itself. The hope fostered in the formation of the Society for Psychical Research was that by investigating these experiences we would be lead to a deeper understanding of ourselves and our place in the world. Ultimately the secrets of anomalous experiences, whether they are purely psychosomatic or are based in material fact, go right to the heart of the secrets of human experience itself. A SOCIETY CONSCIOUS OF THE TRUTH Any sufficiently rigorously defined magic is indistinguishable from technology. —Larry Niven The seventeenth-century philosopher Thomas Vaughan said, “it cannot be thought unreasonable and certainly not unseasonable, if a Society conscious of the Truth, and skilled in the abstruse principles of Nature, shall endeavor to rectify the world: for hitherto we have been abused with Greek Fables and a pretended knowledge of Causes, but without their much desired Effects.” Although the root causes of such phenomena may remain sub rosa, and unattainable by the prying eyes of science and skepticism, those who would approach them in humility, without prejudice, and with a narrative eye for meaning, will find much to explore. The applied mathematician Peter Rothman, who helped developed some of the first streaming media applications for sites such as www.Live365.com , has pointed out that while the futurist and author Arthur C. Clarke’s observation that advanced science begins to look like magic has been hammered into the collective consciousness, there is a flip side to the equation, one articulated by author Larry Niven, which is equally valid. Niven proposed that those things that are considered magical, anomalous, or paranormal are themselves, when properly understood, a subject of true science that takes into account experiential truths whose effects are observable, and under the right conditions repeatable. Repeatability is not a problem when it comes to anomalous experiences. We’ve all had them, whether it’s a synchronicity in repeating numbers, a brief precognitive burst, or some more profound encounter with the other order. In seeking to fit reality to our assumptions, we fall into the dilemma that Vaughan points out, “we have been abused with Greek Fables and a pretended knowledge of Causes, but without their much desired Effects.” EVERYTHING IS TURNED UPSIDE DOWN And when they found them not; they drew Jason and certain brethren unto the rulers of the city, crying, These that have turned the world upside down are come hither. —Acts 17:6 In the magical world everything is turned upside down; the Greek fables that Vaughan mentions are not the rites of Dionysus, the Mysteries of Demeter and Persephone, or Socrates’s daimon, they are the same faulty methods of Galen, Hippocrates, and the other founders of a crippled science so widely denounced by Giordano Bruno, William Blake, and, sadly, by the very methodology of science itself. It is the fable of mathematics divorced from mysticism. We needn’t fall into the error of the skeptic, using tricks of rhetoric and technique to manipulate an exposition that provides false proof of a logical error, observation alone shows that there is more going on with anomalous phenomena than the madness of crowds or personal delusion. As Rothman suggests, our future science is not founded on increasing the cleverness of our automatons, rather it is a relationship with a most profound and personal magic. The ground is already laid for a generation of creative, scientific philosophers to move beyond stalemate arguments. Keel and other active explorers of the theoretical unknown have laid a vast groundwork. We have our critical theory, what we need now is our new Archimedes, whose mathematical models of infinity were recently discovered hidden beneath the devout prayers of the faithful. What we need now are those who will cease dead-end debates and join the active building of a new era of conscious engagement with the mystery of life. What we need now are those who will show up, silhouettes against the sky on a rainy night, and risk being mistaken for the devil himself. 9 PSIVERTS AND PSYCHIC PIRACY: THE FUTURE OF PARAPSYCHOLOGY? DAVID LUKE I woke up this morning with a psychic advert left lingering in my dreaming mind. It was some kind of oneiric flyer for a new type of yoga, it even had a telephone number on it to call. This fanciful hypnopompic intrusion brought me back to the idea that if science can identify techniques for reliably producing psychic abilities (termed “psi”) then public relations executives will soon be pumping millions into pumping adverts directly into our minds. Forget the television, tube trains, and pub toilets, we’ll have adverts (or perhaps
